revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:いざなぎ流]]
'''Izanagi-ryū''' (いざなぎ流) is a unique form of {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|qid=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} and {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|qid=Q1068640|lt=folk religion|1=Folk religion}} passed down in {{ill|Tosa Province|en|Tosa Province|qid=Q857330|lt=Tosa Province|1=Tosa Province}}, specifically in {{ill|Monobe,_Kōchi|en|Monobe,_Kōchi|qid=Q6901336|lt=Monobe, Kōchi|1=Monobe, Kōchi}} (now part of {{ill|Kami, Kōchi|en|Kami, Kōchi|qid=Q877689|lt=Kami City|1=Kami, Kōchi}}, {{ill|Kōchi Prefecture|en|Kōchi Prefecture|qid=Q134093|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|1=Kōchi Prefecture}}).
== Overview ==
Izanagi-ryū is a folk religion that independently developed elements of Onmyōdō in Tosa Province.
According to tradition, it is based on 24 types of magical practices transmitted by Izanagi Daiō from {{ill|Tianzhu (India)|en|Tianzhu (India)|qid=Q3179625|lt=Tenjiku|1=Tianzhu (India)}} ({{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}). The rituals use no permanent tools; instead, they employ specific paper cuttings ('''gohei''') made of {{ill|Washi|en|Washi|qid=Q846299|lt=washi|1=Washi}} tailored to each ceremony. While a folk religion, its liturgical prayers and spells are systematized and formally transmitted.
Ceremonies are conducted by priests called '''tayū''', but these priests are neither hereditary nor part of a specific organizational structure, and there are no restrictions based on gender.<ref name="ftv">[https://www.fujitv.co.jp/b_hp/fnsaward/backnumber/back/01-351.html 10th FNS Documentary Award Nominee: "The Gods of the Frontier – The Cosmos of Izanagi-ryū in Tosa, Oku-Monobe" (produced by Kōchi Sansan TV)]</ref> A suitable person within the community, recognized for their capabilities, inherits the extensive {{ill|saimon|ja|祭文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saimon|1=saimon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17991683}} (ritual texts) and ceremonial styles of Izanagi-ryū.<ref name="ftv"/>
For a long time, no direct historical connection had been established between Izanagi-ryū and the {{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}} or {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}}, the founders of Onmyōji traditions in medieval {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q740246}}. However, a license document issued by the Tsuchimikado family was recently discovered in a private house in Monobe Village. This finding revealed that during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, Izanagi-ryū was officially recognized by the Tsuchimikado family as a local adaptation of Onmyōdō, with tayū serving as approved folk Onmyōji.<ref name="NHK">[https://www.nhk.or.jp/historia/backnumber/320.html NHK History Secrets: "Welcome to the Dark Side of Heian-kyō – The Secrets of Onmyōji Abe no Seimei"]</ref>
== Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property ==
The ceremonial dance, Mai Kagura, is designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6573893}} of Japan as part of {{ill|Tosa Kagura|ja|土佐の神楽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tosa Kagura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes ==
